schools.summary: Ellicott's eulogy: since Moses none so great; Samuel restored the Law and founded the prophetic schools; 'all Israel' gathered, not a fragment.
Henry: Samuel's retirement was God's chastisement of a people who rejected him; hard hearts bury faithful ministers with dry eyes.
Wesley echoes Henry on the duty to grieve for praying ministers; Clarke fixes his age near ninety-eight.
Gill weighs the Jewish chronology of how long Samuel died before Saul, and the fast kept on the twentieth of Ijar.

debates.question: What is meant by burial 'in his house'?

sides.position: a tomb Samuel had built for his own sepulture
the court or garden of his house, to avoid ceremonial defilement

schools.summary: Maon and Carmel fixed in the hill country of Judah; the whole story shows Jehovah watching over David; sheep-shearing was a festal occasion.
Henry: riches make Nabal look great to the world but mean to a right eye; God often gives estates to Nabals without wisdom or grace.
Gill: Nabal dwelt where his business lay, at Carmel among his fields and vineyards.
Wesley: the desert near Carmel would recall God's care for wandering Israel.
Nabal = 'fool' (from naval, to fade); Abigail = 'whose father is joy,' 'the good angel of the household,' her piety far in advance of her age.
Clarke derives Nabal from a root for foolish/base (Latin nebulo) and relishes the LXX 'doggish man'; Wesley: his Caleb descent aggravates his crime.
Gill, with Kimchi, takes Nabal for a nickname fitted to the man's genius and conduct.

debates.question: What does 'of the house of Caleb' mean?

sides.position: a descendant of Caleb (Kalibi), a degenerate heir of a noble stock
from kelev, a dog — 'a doggish, cynical man' (LXX, Syriac, Arabic)

themes: a wise wife, a foolish husband

schools.summary: Ellicott: David's band lived on border contributions and raids; his position was like a friendly Arab sheikh's, and he really protected the land — so Nabal's refusal was worse than churlish.
Wesley: sheep-shearing times were kept with feasting, making the season a fit and generous one for the request.
Gill: David 'heard in the wilderness' — a wilderness indeed to a man in want of food.

themes: the ground of a just claim

schools.summary: Keil reads the embassy as a model of Eastern courtesy: greet, recall the kindness done the shepherds, then ask a present.
Henry: David in effect comes begging, yet begs graciously — choosing the feast-time, calling himself Nabal's 'son,' asking as a favour what he might have claimed.
Gill: ten young men were sent to show honour and reverence to Nabal.
Clarke: the ancient threefold greeting of peace to a man, his house, and his goods, hinting David had protected all; Wesley hears a tacit reminder of David's own distress.
Ellicott: 'to him that liveth' renders an obscure Hebrew idiom (lechai) best taken as a popular congratulation, 'may you prosper.'
Gill: either a contrast — Nabal 'lives' while David starves — or a wish for continued prosperity at next year's shearing.

debates.question: What does the phrase 'to him that liveth in prosperity' (lechai) mean?

sides.position: a popular congratulatory idiom, 'may it go well with you'
a pointed contrast between Nabal's abundance and David's want, or a wish 'for life'

schools.summary: Clarke: David had a real claim — he not only spared Nabal's herdmen but protected them from marauders who would have plundered them; Wesley: Nabal was bound in justice and gratitude to requite it.
Ellicott, with Lange and Ewald: the request was fair; David's band guarded the flocks against raids and had a share in the security that made the feast possible.
Gill: David rehearses the favours done to lay the ground of his petition.
Clarke: a reasonable and modest request that David and his men share the feast they helped make possible; Wesley: a 'good day' when men are most liberal and bound to relieve the needy.
Henry: 'whatsoever cometh to thine hand' — David leaves all to Nabal, asking as a boon to a friend, not as tribute; beggars must not be choosers.
Gill: the young men to be consulted are the very shepherds who could verify David's claim.

themes: leaving the gift to the giver

schools.summary: 'And ceased' is better 'and they sat down' (Keil, Ellicott), waiting for an answer — variously rendered 'waited modestly' (Bunsen) or 'were silent' (Vulgate).
Gill: the messengers came to Carmel and delivered David's words faithfully and exactly.

debates.question: How should the closing verb ('and ceased') be rendered?

sides.position: 'and they sat down,' waiting for a reply
'and they were silent' / 'waited modestly'

schools.summary: Keil: 'who is David?' means 'what have I to do with him?'; Nabal brands him a runaway vagrant to justify his greed. Ellicott: the taunt marks Nabal a partisan of Saul and enemy of David.
Clarke: an unjust refusal made in a highly insulting manner, proving him a son of Belial none can speak to.
Henry: even the Philistines owned David, yet his neighbour affects not to know him and upbraids him with his distress — the vile person speaks villany.
Gill: a haughty, wrathful answer, as covetous churls give when they mean to refuse.

debates.question: Why does David's wrath blaze so fiercely at a mere refusal?

sides.position: chiefly the ingratitude and insulting manner of the denial
Nabal's words reveal him a partisan of Saul and a deadly political enemy

themes: a small denial made a mortal affront

schools.summary: Keil: the verse is one indignant question; Ellicott hears 'men I know not' as a sneer at rebel freebooters, and defends 'water' (against the LXX 'wine') as a precious commodity and a mark of accuracy.
Henry: the fivefold 'my' betrays a man who thinks himself absolute lord of his goods; but we are stewards, not owners — riches are 'another's.'
Gill: bread and water stand for all food and drink; water is named because scarce and precious in the wilderness.

debates.question: Should Nabal's 'my water' be read as 'wine'?

sides.position: retain 'water,' precious in that dry country and a mark of the record's accuracy
the LXX substitutes 'wine,' thinking water an odd thing to hoard

schools.summary: Henry: the messengers rendered no railing for railing nor took by force, but left the cause to David — as the Lord's servants must leave their wrongs to him.
Gill: they turned their backs in silent contempt, doing no injury, resolved only to report the usage to David.
Keil: the churlish reply excited David's anger, and he set out with four hundred men, leaving two hundred with the baggage.
Keil: David's wrath, though intelligible, was a sinful burst of passion from which the Lord preserved him by Abigail; Ellicott: accounted for, though not excused.
Henry: 'Is this thy voice, O David?' — the man who spared Saul now vows to destroy a whole family; what are the best of men when God leaves them to themselves?
Gill: David was provoked to the last degree by so churlish an answer to so humble a message.

debates.question: How far is David's murderous resolve to be judged?

sides.position: a real sin and fall, though under strong provocation — restrained only by God
accounted for by genuine, even political, provocation, yet not excused

schools.summary: Ellicott: the servant, used to Nabal's ungovernable temper, ran to the peace-making Abigail, whose wit saw the deadly danger; Keil: his report frames the whole rescue as the Lord's watch over David.
Gill: the informant was one of Nabal's own shepherds, who knew David and had witnessed the railing.
Henry: the servant did David justice, Nabal no wrong, and the family a kindness by warning the one person able to act.
Ellicott and Keil: a weighty, unsought testimony from Nabal's own foreman — David so disciplined his free lances that they were 'a wall' of defence and a blessing to the country, not a curse.
Gill: David's men were kind, civil, and serviceable, and deserved far better than Nabal gave them.
Gill: David's men were 'a wall' guarding Nabal's flocks against Philistine raiders, plundering Arabs, and wild beasts, by night and day.
Keil: the wall was 'a firm protection round us,' a bulwark against the Bedouin raiders of the desert.
Keil: 'know and see what thou doest, for evil is determined against the whole house'; Ellicott: 'son of Belial' means simply a worthless man, one who cannot be reasoned with.
Gill: the servant appeals to Abigail to act for the preservation of her family, since nothing can be done with Nabal.

themes: the crisis given to the wise wife

schools.summary: Keil itemizes the present sent ahead on asses; Ellicott defends the modest quantities against the LXX and Ewald as a liberal gift for David's immediate followers, prepared in haste.
Henry: the virtuous wife acts quickly and herself; a gift pacifies anger (as Jacob pacified Esau); she disposed of Nabal's goods lawfully and laudably for the family's defence.
Clarke: the present was small — five sheep out of three thousand — and would have satisfied David at first; Wesley defends her acting without leave in a case of necessity.
Gill: she made haste, her family being in imminent danger.

debates.question: Were the quantities of the present too small?

sides.position: the text is right — a liberal gift for David's immediate band, prepared in haste
the LXX and Ewald enlarge the figures, thinking them too small for David's host

themes: the open hand against the closed fist

schools.summary: Gill: she sent the present ahead so the sight of it would pacify David and prepare him to hear her.
Keil: she followed behind to appease him, coming down by a hidden part of the mountain; the narrator inserts David's vow as a parenthesis.
Ellicott, following Keil: 'the covert of the hill' is a hollow between two peaks, where the two parties, each descending, meet.
Gill: Abigail rode down after her servants, the present going first and its mistress close behind.
Keil and Ellicott: a parenthetical clause baring David's mind — Nabal repaid faithful protection with insult, evil for good, and this is David's self-justification for the vengeance he intends.
Gill: David was saying this to himself or his men at the very moment Abigail met him.
Henry: though requited evil for good, we should not repent of good done, for God is kind to the evil and unthankful.

themes: a just grievance curdling into revenge

schools.summary: Clarke: nothing can justify the vow to cut off a whole innocent family — abominable and cruel; David himself will condemn it. Wesley: 'enemies of David' means David himself, softened to avoid self-cursing; is this the voice of David?

Keil: 'any that pisseth against the wall' means every male; the oath oddly falls on 'David's enemies.' Ellicott: the Hebrew is right; a superstitious dread of self-cursing lies behind it, and the rash oath is not binding.

debates.question: How should David's murderous oath be judged?

sides.position: utterly unjustifiable, cruel, even diabolic — David suffered no real wrong
the sinful excess of a truly provoked man, and a rash oath better broken than kept

schools.summary: Ellicott: her obeisance betrays her sense that she addresses the Lord's anointed and future king; she moves to deprecate his wrath and excuse her husband. Keil: she falls at his feet as a suppliant.
Henry: she takes the posture of penitent and petitioner before servants and soldiers alike — 'yielding pacifies great offences.'
Gill: she knew David, or guessed him by the armed company, and did him immediate reverence.
Keil: she takes the guilt on herself, hoping David will not avenge it upon her, before unfolding her arguments for pardon.
Wesley: 'impute Nabal's sin to me' — an absolute submission to mercy without justification, using every argument the greatest orator could employ.
Henry: generous spirits bear the faults of others; taking the blame proved her conjugal love — 'whatever Nabal was, he was her husband.'

themes: the innocent bearing the guilty's debt

schools.summary: Henry: she excuses Nabal by his want of understanding — folly, not malice — echoing 'he knows not what he does,' and pleads her own ignorance of the messengers.
Keil, with Schmidt: 'a wise man should pardon a fool'; she prays David to take no notice of Nabal.
Gill and Wesley: Nabal's name means 'fool,' and his wife turns it into her plea — his rudeness is folly beneath a great man's revenge.

Ellicott: render it 'it is the Lord that hath withholden thee'; she speaks as though the danger were past and David had cause to thank God for being kept from blood-guilt. Keil: only third does she mention the present, for the young men.
Gill: her certainty rested on an inward impulse and on the softening she could read in David's countenance.
Wesley: 'let thine enemies be as Nabal' — as contemptible and powerless, forced to yield and beg pardon.

Ellicott: 'this blessing' is the gift, brought as homage in the Eastern manner but directed to David's company, not himself; Keil: berakah is used of a present (as Jacob's to Esau), laid 'at the feet of' the young men.
Wesley: a gift is a 'blessing' both as coming from God's blessing and as given with good will; she offers it as unworthy of David's own use.
Gill: the present came as a blessing from God and with good will from her.

themes: routing the gift to the servants

schools.summary: Keil: her words almost amount to prophecy of David's 'sure house' and divine election; Ellicott hears words learned in the prophetic schools, fulfilled ultimately in Christ, and glosses 'the battles of the Lord.'
Wesley and Clarke: warring for the Lord is David's proper work, not private revenge; 'evil hath not been found in thee' — he must not now justify his enemies' reproaches by cruelty.
Gill: she asks forgiveness of the trespass — Nabal's, which she has made her own, or her own boldness.

Keil: the 'bundle of life' is drawn from binding up valuables, referring first to earthly preservation, then to eternal life; she courteously avoids naming Saul. Ellicott gathers the Talmud and links it to Revelation 6:9.
Wesley and Clarke: David's life is precious, bound safe with God, his enemies slung away with violence; the phrase became a Jewish gravestone hope of eternal life.
Gill: 'a man is risen' means Saul, whom she chose not to name.

debates.question: Does 'the bundle of life' mean earthly preservation or eternal life?

sides.position: primarily the safe keeping of the righteous on earth, though implying eternal life
a definite early expression of belief in an eternal future with God

themes: God keeps his own as treasure

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: Abigail's certainty of David's kingship implies knowledge from Samuel or the prophetic schools, whose teachers had spread through the land — a maturity of faith bred by intercourse with prophets.
Gill: 'all the good he hath spoken' means God's performing his promise concerning David's kingdom.
Keil: the pardon will be no stumbling-block or anguish of heart when David is king; Ellicott: she bids him keep a conscience with no shed blood to look back on, and remember her then.
Wesley: the deed would be causeless (Nabal deserving no death, his family innocent) and against God's law forbidding private vengeance.
Henry: her last and strongest argument — the sweetness of revenge soon turns to bitterness; spare the conscience a deed it will wish undone.
Henry: Abigail a wise reprover, David an obedient ear ('let the righteous smite me'); we must see God sending those who meet us with reproof, and thank him for providences that prevent sin.
Keil: her words appeased his wrath; he blessed the Lord for sending her before turning to congratulate her, else he had taken his revenge.
Gill: David, overcome by her rhetoric and arguments, breaks into praise.

themes: the wise reprover and obedient ear

schools.summary: Ellicott: David frankly owns he was in the wrong and would have stained his fame but for Abigail; and a calmer age has learned that a rash oath is better broken than kept (unlike Saul and Jephthah).
Henry: few take reproof thankfully; the nearer to sin, the greater the mercy of a seasonable restraint.
Gill: 'thy advice' is 'thy taste' — her discretion and understanding, which David blesses.

themes: the non-obligation of a rash oath

schools.summary: Henry: David now sees the greatness of the sin (blood-guilt, self-avenging that usurps God's throne) and the nearness of the fall — the nearer to sin, the greater the mercy of restraint.
Keil: the strongly adversative 'nevertheless' — but for her haste the sworn slaughter would have fallen by morning.
Gill: David confirms his words with a solemn oath, the form now turned from threat to confession of rescue.
Keil: David received the gifts and dismissed her with assurance of pardon; 'accepted thy person' is the idiom of gracious acceptance. Wesley: he showed acceptance by granting her request.
Henry: David owns himself overcome — good men hear reason from inferiors; oaths cannot bind to sin; a faithful reproof speeds better than expected.
Gill: he kindly took the present, 'a present for a prince,' as the seal of peace.
Henry: sinners are most secure when most in danger; Nabal, extravagant and sottishly drunk, could not be counselled — good advice to the drunk is pearls before swine.
Keil: a king's feast, drunken above measure, so she told him nothing till morning; Ellicott: it completes the portrait of the churlish sheep-master against his high-minded wife.
Wesley: covetousness and prodigality met in him; drunk, his reason and conscience were asleep, past admonition.
Gill: Abigail returned to find him at a great feast.
Keil: sobered and told, he was smitten with a stroke, his heart turning to stone — from fear, alarm, and vexation at being humbled; Clarke: thunderstruck and insensible, he lingered ten days.
Henry: his heart merry with wine overnight, heavy as a stone by morning — the laughter of the fool is transient, carnal pleasures deceitful.
Gill: Abigail waited till he was sober and capable of understanding.

sides.position: terror at the danger he had so narrowly escaped
a burst of rage that his wife had humbled herself before David

schools.summary: Keil: the Lord ended his life by a second stroke ten days later; Ellicott holds the natural cause (apoplexy) and the divine 'shaft' together — Scripture calls disease God's arrow.
Henry: the rescue of the wicked is but a reservation for sorer wrath; just that the graceless die without comfort — and none lamented Nabal.
Gill: he lay ten days senseless before the end came.

debates.question: Was Nabal's death a direct divine stroke or natural causes?

sides.position: the Lord smote him — a judgment on his sin
natural apoplexy, which Scripture nonetheless calls a shaft of the Most High

schools.summary: Keil: David praised the Lord for avenging his shame and holding him from self-revenge; Clarke: he was now free to marry Abigail and gained Nabal's estate.
Henry: David blesses God for keeping him from killing Nabal, for vindicating his honour, and for teaching him to commit his cause to God.
Wesley: David's blessing shows 'another instance of human infirmity,' and the marriage came some time after, not at once.

debates.question: Is David's blessing of God at Nabal's death wholly proper?

sides.position: pure thanksgiving that God avenged him and kept his hands clean
tinged with the very vindictiveness he had just renounced — a human infirmity

schools.summary: Keil: she consents without hesitation, professing herself fit only to wash the feet of David's servants; Ellicott: the interval since Nabal's death is unspecified but may be long.
Ambrose (via Ellicott) allegorises the marriage as the union of the Church — the Gentile world — to Christ after its break with heathenism.
Gill: Abigail was found at Carmel, where she perhaps stayed after Nabal's death rather than at Maon.

outliers.note: Latin father who allegorises Abigail's espousal to David as the Church (the Gentiles) wedded to Christ after leaving heathenism.

schools.summary: Keil: deep Eastern reverence — she is ready to wash the feet of David's very servants — and then rises hastily to follow the messengers.
Gill: she bowed to the earth before the messengers as representing David, honouring them as if he were present.
Henry: she married David in faith of God's promise, not questioning his poverty — as those joined to Christ suffer with him now, believing they shall reign with him.
Gill: she consented without hesitation; his poverty was no obstacle, for she believed he would be king and could supply him till then.
Keil: she rose hastily and followed the messengers with five damsels, a retinue befitting her rank.

themes: the fit partner of the king

schools.summary: Keil fixes Jezreel in the hill country of Judah; Ellicott names the fatal fruits of polygamy — intrigues, crimes, and murders in David's later household.
Henry: David was carried away by a corrupt custom — 'from the beginning it was not so' — occasioned perhaps by Saul's theft of Michal.
Clarke: Ahinoam, always named first and mother of Amnon, was likely married before Abigail.
Gill: takes Ahinoam to have become David's wife before Abigail.

debates.question: Was Ahinoam married before or after Abigail?

sides.position: before Abigail, since she is named first and bore the eldest son
the text simply records both as David's wives

themes: the bitter fruits in his house

schools.summary: Keil: Saul took Michal from David and gave her to Palti of Gallim (later restored to David); Ellicott: a fixed resolve to break with David and to attach Phalti to Saul's cause.
Gill: Saul acted to vex David, to break the son-in-law tie, and to bar any claim it might give him to the throne.
Henry: Michal, the wife of David's youth, was given away in token of Saul's displeasure, disclaiming the father-in-law's bond.

debates.question: Why did Saul give Michal to another?

sides.position: personal spite — to vex David and dissolve the kinship that shielded him
cold policy — a fixed break with David and a bid to win Phalti

themes: a broken bond beside two new ones
